questions.question: Which command stages all changes within a specific directory?
What does 'git add .' do?
options: Stages only the files in the root directory
Stages everything in the current directory and below
Commits all changes in the current directory
Deletes all untracked files in the current directory
questions.answer: Stages everything in the current directory and below

questions.question: Which command would you use to stage all '.txt' files?
What does 'git add -p' do?
options: Stages all files in the project
Pushes staged changes to the remote
Interactively choose which hunks to stage
Prints the list of staged files
questions.answer: Interactively choose which hunks to stage

questions.question: Which command unstages a file while keeping the changes in the working directory?
After running 'git restore --staged temp2.txt', what happens to temp2.txt?
options: The file is deleted from disk
The file is removed from the staging area but still exists in the working directory
The file is pushed to the remote repository
questions.answer: The file is removed from the staging area but still exists in the working directory

questions.question: How do you unstage all currently staged files at once?
What is the recommended maximum length for the first line of a commit message?
What should a good commit message explain?
options: Why a change was made, not just what was changed
The exact lines of code that were modified
questions.answer: Why a change was made, not just what was changed

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a BAD commit message?
options: Fix off-by-one error in loop boundary check
Add input validation to user registration form

questions.question: In a multi-line commit message, what should the optional longer description be wrapped at?
When using 'git log --oneline', which part of the commit message is shown?
options: Only the first line of the commit message
Only the last line of the commit message
questions.answer: Only the first line of the commit message

questions.question: What does 'git commit --amend -m "new message"' do?
options: Creates a brand new commit with the new message
Replaces the message of the most recent commit
Appends the new message to the most recent commit message
Changes the message of every commit in the log
questions.answer: Replaces the message of the most recent commit

questions.question: What happens to the commit hash when you amend a commit?
options: The commit hash stays the same

questions.question: What does the '--no-edit' flag do when used with 'git commit --amend'?
options: Prevents any files from being added to the commit
Keeps the same commit message without opening an editor
Prevents the commit from being created
questions.answer: Keeps the same commit message without opening an editor

questions.question: Why should you avoid amending commits that have already been pushed to a shared remote?
options: It will delete the remote repository
Amending rewrites history, which causes problems for collaborators
The remote server will reject all future pushes
It converts the repository to read-only mode
questions.answer: Amending rewrites history, which causes problems for collaborators

questions.question: You have staged both temp1.txt and temp2.txt. You want to commit only temp1.txt. What should you do first?
options: Delete temp2.txt from the working directory
Run 'git commit temp1.txt' to skip temp2.txt
Run 'git rm temp2.txt' to remove it

questions.question: You created files in both src/ and docs/ directories. You want to commit them separately. What is the correct approach?
options: Run 'git add .' and commit everything at once
Stage src/ first, commit, then stage docs/ and commit
Delete the docs/ files, commit src/, then recreate docs/
Use 'git commit --split' to separate the directories
questions.answer: Stage src/ first, commit, then stage docs/ and commit

questions.question: What is the older command for unstaging a file, equivalent to 'git restore --staged <file>'?
After running 'git restore --staged .' to unstage everything, what is the state of the files?
options: All files are deleted from disk
All files still exist on disk but are no longer staged
All files are reverted to their last committed version
questions.answer: All files still exist on disk but are no longer staged

questions.question: Which of the following is a GOOD commit message?
options: Add input validation to user registration form
questions.answer: Add input validation to user registration form

questions.question: You just committed a file but forgot to include critical2.txt in that commit. What commands add it to the last commit without changing the message?
options: git add critical2.txt && git commit -m "add forgotten file"

questions.question: You want to stage only files matching 'test_results_*.txt' but not 'meeting_notes.txt'. Which command achieves this?
What does 'git log -1' display?
options: The first commit ever made in the repository
The most recent single commit with full details
A list of all commits on one line each
Only the commit hash of the latest commit
questions.answer: The most recent single commit with full details

questions.question: In the multi-line commit message format, what separates the short summary from the longer description?
